message.content:
## Source Extractor

Research question: "DESIGNING AND TRAINING A VOICE / TTS MODEL TO BE MORE COMPREHENSIBLE AT HIGH-SPEED LISTENING. Deliver BOTH the underlying science AND concrete recommendations for a local open-source TTS podcast pipeline (currently uses Kokoro TTS). Cover these 6 angles: (1) ACOUSTIC/PROSODIC PROPERTIES that survive time-compression / make a voice intelligible when sped up — "clear speech"/hyperarticulation, vowel-space expansion, consonant emphasis/sharper consonant-vowel transitions, modulation-spectrum shape, F0/pitch characteristics, and pause/rhythm structure. (2) FORMANT SYNTHESIS vs NEURAL TTS at extreme speed — why blind screen-reader power users overwhelmingly prefer old formant synthesizers (Eloquence, DECtalk, eSpeak-NG) over natural neural voices at 18-22 syllables/second; consistency, crispness, latency, predictability; quantify the naturalness-vs-intelligibility trade-off. (3) RATE-AWARE / CONTROLLABLE TTS GENERATION — conditioning or fine-tuning a neural TTS to NATIVELY generate fast-but-clear speech (speaking-rate control, prosody control, training on clear-speech/Lombard-speech corpora) vs. post-hoc time-scale modification; CRITICAL: how much MEASURABLE comprehensibility gain does native fast generation or fine-tuning actually buy over simply time-compressing a normal voice? (the user will only invest in fine-tuning/training if the gain is significant). (4) DSP ENHANCEMENT for intelligibility at speed — formant/clarity enhancement, spectral shaping + dynamic-range compression, consonant boosting, content-aware PER-PHONEME variable-rate compression (compress steady-state vowels more, preserve consonant transitions), and modulation-envelope enhancement; these apply to ANY engine's output. (5) CONCRETE ENGINES/VOICES & CONFIG KNOBS — head-to-head comparison of which TTS engines/voices are most intelligible at high speed (Kokoro, Piper, eSpeak-NG/Eloquence, RHVoice, Azure/Google neural, ElevenLabs) and the tunable settings (speaking rate, pitch, pause length) that improve speed-comprehensibility. (6) HOW TO MEASURE "comprehensibility at speed" — objective intelligibility metrics (STOI, STMI/spectro-temporal modulation index, modulation-spectrum analysis) and practical subjective A/B / MUSHRA listening-test protocols, so one can decide whether a fine-tune cleared a "significant gain" bar before committing. CONSTRAINTS/CONTEXT: pipeline runs LOCALLY/on-device (open-source Kokoro), so favor approaches compatible with local inference. Output should serve TWO use cases: a normal natural-sounding default voice for general students, AND an optional aggressively speed-optimized track for trained power listeners (robotic is acceptable for that track if measurably clearer). For EACH effort tier (pick/configure a voice < DSP post-processing < fine-tune an open model < train from scratch), state the expected comprehensibility gain, the cost/effort, and whether it is worth it. Prefer peer-reviewed and authoritative sources; include practitioner/community knowledge from the blind/screen-reader speed-listening community where relevant."

Fetch and extract key claims from this source:
**URL:** https://journals.plos.org/ploscompbiol/article?id=10.1371%2Fjournal.pcbi.1000302
**Title:** The Modulation Transfer Function for Speech Intelligibility (Elliott & Theunissen, PLoS Comput Biol)
**Found via:** acoustic-science/clear-speech search

## Task
1. Use WebFetch to retrieve the page content.
2. Assess source quality: primary research/institution? secondary reporting? blog/opinion? forum? unreliable?
3. Extract 2-5 FALSIFIABLE claims that bear on the research question. Each claim must:
   - be a concrete, checkable statement (not vague generalities)
   - include a direct quote from the source as support
   - be rated central/supporting/tangential to the research question
4. Note publish date if available.

If the fetch fails or the page is irrelevant/paywalled, return claims: [] and sourceQuality: "unreliable".

Structured output only.

attachment.content:
- caveman: Ultra-compressed communication mode. Cuts token usage ~75% by speaking like caveman while keeping full technical accuracy. Supports intensity levels: lite, full (default), ultra, wenyan-lite, wenyan-full, wenyan-ultra. Use when user says "caveman mode", "talk like caveman", "use caveman", "less tokens", "be brief", or invokes /caveman. Also auto-triggers when token efficiency is requested.
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- frontend-design:frontend-design: Create distinctive, production-grade frontend interfaces with high design quality. Use this skill when the user asks to build web components, pages, or applications. Generates creative, polished code that avoids generic AI aesthetics.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll fetch the source and extract relevant claims.

input.prompt: Extract key findings about the modulation transfer function (MTF) for speech intelligibility. Specifically: (1) which spectral and temporal modulation frequencies are most important for speech intelligibility, (2) the core spectro-temporal modulation regions (in cycles/octave for spectral and Hz for temporal) that carry intelligibility, (3) any quantitative thresholds or peak modulation values, (4) implications for time-compression / sped-up speech and modulation-spectrum shaping. Provide direct verbatim quotes for each finding. Also report the publication date and authors.

content.content:
# Key Findings on Speech Intelligibility Modulation Transfer Function

**Publication:** March 6, 2009
**Authors:** Taffeta M. Elliott and Frédéric E. Theunissen

## Critical Spectral and Temporal Modulation Frequencies

**Spectral domain (low-pass):**
> "Comprehension was significantly impaired when temporal modulations <12 Hz or spectral modulations <4 cycles/kHz were removed."

**Temporal domain (band-pass):**
> "temporal modulations from 1 to 7 Hz and spectral modulations <1 cycles/kHz were the most important"

## Core Spectro-Temporal Regions

The researchers identified a critical "core" region below approximately 3.75 cycles/kHz (spectral) and 7.75 Hz (temporal), achieving 75% word recognition when limited to these modulations.

**Detailed temporal tuning:**
> "intermediate temporal modulations between 1 and 7 Hz are critical for speech intelligibility"

**Overall MTF shape:**
> "The human speech intelligibility transfer function appears therefore to show a band-pass tuning in the temporal domain and a low-pass tuning in the spectral domain."

## Implications for Time-Compression

The bandpass temporal tuning (1-7 Hz) aligns with syllable rates (2-5 Hz), suggesting aggressive time-compression removing faster modulations would degrade intelligibility more than slower compression affecting only pitch-related frequencies.

content.text: The fetch was successful. Let me extract one more detail about the noise/quantitative aspects to ensure I have accurate claims.

input.prompt: Report verbatim any sentences describing: the percentage of word recognition achieved when speech was restricted to the most important "core" modulation region; the precise upper bounds of the core region in cycles/kHz and Hz; and any statement about how much of the modulation spectrum can be removed while preserving intelligibility. Quote exactly.

content.content:
# Speech Intelligibility Core Modulation Results

**Word Recognition Performance:**
"Comprehension of core modulations was 75% word recognition"

**Core Region Boundaries:**
The core modulation region was defined by low-pass filtering at "3.75 cycles/kHz and <7.75 Hz"

**Spectrum Removal and Intelligibility:**
The research demonstrates substantial redundancy in the speech signal. When intermediate spectral and temporal modulations outside the core region were removed through notch filtering, "the intelligibility of most notch-filtered stimuli remained excellent" even at challenging noise conditions (2 dB SNR). This indicates that a significant portion of the modulation spectrum can be eliminated while preserving intelligible speech.

content.text: I have sufficient information to extract the claims.

claims.claim: Human speech intelligibility shows band-pass tuning in the temporal modulation domain (1-7 Hz most important) and low-pass tuning in the spectral modulation domain, defining the modulation regions that must be preserved for comprehensible speech.
claims.quote: The human speech intelligibility transfer function appears therefore to show a band-pass tuning in the temporal domain and a low-pass tuning in the spectral domain.

claims.claim: Intermediate temporal modulations between 1 and 7 Hz are the most critical band for speech intelligibility; since these correspond to syllable rate, time-compressing speech shifts energy out of this band and degrades comprehensibility.
claims.quote: intermediate temporal modulations between 1 and 7 Hz are critical for speech intelligibility

claims.claim: Comprehension is significantly impaired when temporal modulations below 12 Hz or spectral modulations below 4 cycles/kHz are removed, identifying concrete cutoff thresholds for modulation-spectrum shaping aimed at preserving intelligibility.
claims.quote: Comprehension was significantly impaired when temporal modulations <12 Hz or spectral modulations <4 cycles/kHz were removed.

claims.claim: Restricting speech to a 'core' low-pass modulation region of 3.75 cycles/kHz spectral and 7.75 Hz temporal still yields 75% word recognition, quantifying the minimal modulation content needed for intelligible speech.
claims.quote: Comprehension of core modulations was 75% word recognition

claims.claim: The speech signal carries substantial modulation redundancy: notch-filtering out modulations outside the core region leaves intelligibility excellent even at 2 dB SNR, implying much of the modulation spectrum can be removed without harming comprehension.
claims.quote: the intelligibility of most notch-filtered stimuli remained excellent

content.text: Extracted claims from the Elliott & Theunissen (2009) PLoS Computational Biology paper, a peer-reviewed primary research source. The source is highly relevant to research angles (1) acoustic/prosodic properties and modulation-spectrum shape, (4) DSP modulation-envelope enhancement, and (6) modulation-spectrum-based intelligibility measurement. Key takeaway for the pipeline: speech intelligibility depends on a band-pass temporal modulation region of roughly 1-7 Hz (syllable rate) and low-pass spectral modulations, so naive time-compression that pushes energy above this band predictably reduces comprehensibility — supporting content-aware variable-rate compression that preserves the core modulation band.
